query: How does the CSS cascade decide which style wins?

competitors.label: Cascade
competitors.selectionReason: This is MDN's canonical cascade guide and the strongest official baseline for origin, importance, specificity, and source order.
theirStrengths: MDN is authoritative on the full cascade algorithm and the order in which the browser resolves conflicts.
theirGaps: The guide is broader and less compressed around one app-versus-library override story.

realWorldUseCases:
Practical scenario
A component library sets button styles, and your app needs overrides. The cascade order determines whether your app styles actually win.

Common pitfalls
  • Assuming later rules always win without checking specificity.
  • Overusing !important and making future overrides impossible.
  • Forgetting inline styles beat most stylesheet rules.
Trade-off or test tip
Lower specificity is easier to maintain but may lose to library styles. Use DevTools to inspect the winning rule in the computed panel.
followUpConfusion: When multiple rules target the same element, CSS evaluates them using a four-step hierarchy: importance, origin, specificity, and order of appearance.

realWorldUseCases: MDN explains the full cascade precisely, but it is less focused on one compact component-library override scenario.
actionableExamples: It includes strong algorithm explanations and examples, so the example criterion remains close.
followUpConfusion: MDN covers the full algorithm deeply, but our page is more concise about the common misunderstanding that later rules always win.

actionableExamples: Add one before-versus-after DevTools style-pane example where specificity beats source order.

competitors.winReason: MDN is the canonical cascade reference, but our page is more reusable as an interview answer because it turns the algorithm into a real override-debugging scenario and highlights the most common false assumption about source order.

competitors.label: CSS Interview Questions

theirGaps: The landing page is broad and less explicit about the exact four-step cascade order and the library-override debugging story.

realWorldUseCases: A component library sets button styles, and your app needs overrides.
followUpConfusion: Assuming later rules always win without checking specificity.

competitors.label: Don't Fight the Cascade, Control It!
competitors.selectionReason: This CSS-Tricks article is the closest topic-fit tutorial in the locked pool because it focuses on understanding and intentionally controlling the cascade rather than fighting it with ad hoc overrides.
theirStrengths: The article is strong on cascade strategy and on avoiding the reflex to pile on more specificity.
theirGaps: It is less explicit about the exact four-step decision path that answers this trivia question directly.

realWorldUseCases: The cascade order determines whether your app styles actually win.
followUpConfusion: Assuming later rules always win without checking specificity.

realWorldUseCases: CSS-Tricks frames the cascade as something to control deliberately, but it is less compact about one app-versus-library override example.
actionableExamples: It provides strong strategy guidance, so the example criterion remains close.
followUpConfusion: The article is less explicit than our page about the exact order of importance, origin, specificity, and source order.
